summary:
The Revolt of the Satraps (366–360 BC) was a major uprising of western Anatolian provincial governors against the Achaemenid Persian Empire — the most serious internal challenge the empire faced before [[event:75|Alexander the Great's Empire]]'s conquest — which demonstrated both the empire's vulnerability and its resilience in surviving such threats.

The revolt involved multiple satrapies in western Anatolia (including Lydia, Hellespontine Phrygia, and Cappadocia) acting in varying degrees of coordination against the central government under Artaxerxes II. The underlying causes included Persian demands for higher tribute during ongoing wars with Egypt, resentment of court favorites, and the ambitions of powerful provincial governors who had accumulated substantial wealth and private armies. Egypt's pharaoh Tachos provided financial support and even joined the revolt personally. Sparta also intervened, sending the general Agesilaus to assist. Despite this formidable coalition, the revolt collapsed due to the mutual suspicion of the rebel leaders and Persian success in peeling away individual rebels through bribery and diplomacy. The revolt's failure confirmed that the Achaemenid system of divide-and-rule could survive serious challenges — though it would eventually succumb to the unified force of Macedon under [[event:1035|Battle of Gaugamela]].
